title: Singin' hinnies
subtitle: Northumberland griddle scones with lard and currants
excerpt: Singin' hinnies are flat griddle scones from Northumberland, cooked on a heavy pan until the fat in them sizzles and sings. Made with plain flour, lard, currants, and a little milk, they are eaten hot with butter. The name is affectionate Geordie dialect.
sourceNotes: Traditional Northumbrian and Tyneside griddle cake. Singin' hinnies take their name from the sizzling sound they make on the griddle ('singing') and from 'hinny', a Geordie term of endearment. They are closely related to Welsh cakes and other British griddle scones. A regional speciality documented from at least the 19th century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked and cooled between layers of baking paper for up to 2 months. Reheat in a dry pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten hot from the pan. Can be reheated in a dry frying pan.

content.text: Singin' hinnies are rubbed in the same way as a scone: the fat is worked into the flour until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Lard gives the authentic flavour and a lighter texture; butter can be used instead. They are rolled thin, cut into rounds, and cooked slowly on a heavy dry pan. Too much heat and the outside burns before the inside cooks through.

items.prepNote: or unsalted butter, cold and cubed

content.text: Combine the flour, baking powder, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Add the cold lard or butter and rub it in with your fingertips until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs.
Stir in the currants. Add the milk a little at a time, mixing until the dough just comes together. It should not be sticky. Do not overwork it.
On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough out to about 5 mm thick. Cut into rounds using a glass or cutter, about 7 to 8 cm in diameter.

Heat a heavy frying pan over a medium-low heat. Do not add fat. Cook the hinnies for 4 to 5 minutes per side until golden brown and cooked through. The fat in the scone will sizzle gently as they cook. Serve immediately with butter.

items.symptom: The outside is dark but the inside is raw
items.fix: Keep the heat at medium-low and cook slowly. They need 4 to 5 minutes per side, not 2.

items.symptom: The dough crumbles and will not hold together
items.cause: Too little milk, or fat not rubbed in finely enough.
items.fix: Add the milk in small increments and mix gently. If it crumbles in the pan, the dough needed more liquid.

items.cause: Dough was overworked after adding the milk.
items.fix: Mix just until the dough comes together and then stop. Handle it as little as possible.

content.text: Singin' hinnies come from Tyneside and Northumberland and are part of the same family of British griddle breads as Welsh cakes, Scottish drop scones, and Irish soda farls. The word 'hinny' is Geordie dialect for 'honey', used as a term of endearment throughout the North-East. The 'singing' refers to the fat sizzling in the pan. They are still made in Northumberland homes and sold at farmers' markets in the region, though they have never become as widely known as Welsh cakes.
